text: This is a fifty-nine-year-old active male with an incarcerated left-sided direct inguinal hernia. He was seen in the office. Had symptoms only for a few weeks. You can see working lateral to medial as always, no change here. The medial umbilical ligaments seem to be quite different in every population for whatever reason.

The surgeon creates a preperitoneal flap by dissecting the medial and lateral leaflets of the peritoneum, which converge at the inferior epigastric vessels. The surgeon uses electrocautery to push the fat laterally, visibly separating it from the underlying structures.

The surgeon identifies crossing vessels near the inferior epigastric vessels and uses cautery to gently push the antisac away, avoiding aggressive manipulation to protect the iliohypogastric nerve. The dissection proceeds down to the pubic tubercle and Cooper's ligament, staying close to the left iliac vein with minimal extra fat visible, maintaining proximity to the pubic bone.

The surgeon pulls the hernia sac down at an oblique angle beyond the symphysis pubis on the contralateral side, creating a rent in the peritoneal flap. The vas deferens comes into view near the iliac vein, requiring careful attention to avoid buckling the mesh. The surgeon checks for a cord lipoma while maneuvering below the iliac vein.

The surgeon positions the mesh with just enough room, navigating between the coronamortis vein and the iliac vein. Triangular fixation is performed, followed by high medial and high lateral fixation. The peritoneal flap is closed in a standard fashion, aligning the medial umbilical ligament and the preperitoneal fat plane.
